headline: Cybersecurity Leads a Broadly Positive Tuesday as Cool Inflation and Bank Beats Set the Tone
body_html:
Equities closed higher on Tuesday, July 14, as softer-than-expected inflation data combined with encouraging results from major bank earnings gave buyers the confidence to push both large-cap indices into the green. Advancing issues outpaced decliners by 209 to 185 across our 396-name universe — a modest but real tilt to the upside that reflected cautious optimism rather than outright exuberance.

The session's clearest rotation story was the surge in cybersecurity and semiconductor names against a sharp retreat in healthcare. Semiconductors led all sectors, rising 2.23%, while cybersecurity names dominated the individual leaderboard. CrowdStrike jumped 12.14%, Okta climbed 10.81%, Qualys added 8.30%, and Palo Alto Networks gained 6.84% — a sweep credited in part to a positive read-through from IBM's quarterly disclosures. On the other side of the ledger, Healthcare tumbled 1.80%, with Biogen shedding 8.17% and Intuitive Surgical falling 6.78%, dragging medical-device names broadly lower. Consumer stocks also struggled, off 0.94%.

The sector divergence underscores a market that remains highly theme-driven: capital chased digital-infrastructure security while rotating away from defensive healthcare. With geopolitical noise around the Strait of Hormuz appearing to ease after diplomatic overtures toward Gulf investment agreements, energy held a constructive 0.77% gain — a reminder that macro headlines can shift quickly and portfolios must stay nimble.

briefs.headline: IBM Catalyst Ignites a Cybersecurity Sweep
briefs.body: A read-through from IBM's latest results lit a fire under the security software complex on Tuesday. CrowdStrike, Okta, Qualys, and Zscaler all posted gains between roughly 7% and 12%, suggesting investors interpreted IBM's commentary as a broad endorsement of enterprise security spending trends heading into the back half of 2026.

briefs.headline: Hormuz Fee Idea Quietly Shelved in Favour of Gulf Deals
briefs.body: President Trump reversed course on a proposal to charge fees for passage through the Strait of Hormuz, pivoting instead toward Gulf investment agreements. The about-face calmed crude-market nerves and contributed to energy stocks posting a solid 0.77% session gain, even as geopolitical tension in the region showed no sign of fully abating.

briefs.headline: Healthcare Devices Take the Day's Hardest Hit
briefs.body: Intuitive Surgical fell 6.78% and Stryker dropped 6.15%, making medical-device names the steepest individual decliners within an already weak Healthcare sector that lost 1.80% on the day. The broad-based selling suggested sector-specific pressure rather than any single company catalyst, with the healthcare group finishing as Tuesday's worst-performing corner of the market.

briefs.headline: Biogen Slides More Than 8% to Lead Biotech Lower
briefs.body: Biogen shed 8.17%, the steepest single-name decline of the session, adding to pressure across the pharma-biotech space. The move dragged the wider healthcare complex and highlighted an ongoing reluctance among investors to maintain full exposure to large-cap drug names amid an uncertain regulatory and pipeline backdrop.

briefs.headline: Iran Pivots to Red Sea as a New Pressure Point
briefs.body: After the Hormuz fee plan faded, reports emerged that Iran is eyeing the Red Sea corridor as a fresh lever for geopolitical leverage. The development kept an undercurrent of tension in shipping and energy markets even as diplomats worked on separate Lebanon-Israel talks in Rome brokered by Washington.

briefs.headline: EU Aviation Authority Reinstates Middle East Flight Warning
briefs.body: Europe's aviation safety body restored its advisory cautioning carriers over Middle East airspace after a renewed flare-up in regional fighting. The warning adds logistical cost and route uncertainty for European airlines and could weigh on travel-related demand across the corridor if hostilities persist into the peak summer travel season.
